section.title: Prosperity Without Enjoyment
section.sectionRationale: Chapter five ended with the gift of enjoyment; chapter six shows its terrible opposite. Here is a man who has everything, riches, wealth, honor, a house full of children, long years, and lacks nothing his heart desires, except the one thing that would make it worth having: the power to enjoy it. The Teacher calls this a sickness that weighs on mankind, presses it to a shocking comparison with a stillborn child, and closes by confessing how little we can see, name, or secure of our own future.
section.thesis: To possess everything yet lack the God given power to enjoy it is the bitterest futility of all, exposing that our deepest hunger cannot be filled by prosperity, and that we cannot contend with the God who alone gives the ability to be satisfied.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the sickness of prosperity a man cannot enjoy, given instead to a stranger; the dark comparison with a stillborn child who has more rest than the unsatisfied man; and the confession of our limits, the appetite never filled, the future we cannot see under the sun.

units.label: Prosperity a man cannot enjoy
units.note: a sickness that weighs on mankind

verses.text: There is another evil I have seen under the sun, and it weighs heavily upon mankind:

God gives a man riches, wealth, and honor, so that he lacks nothing his heart desires; but God does not allow him to enjoy them. Instead, a stranger will enjoy them. This is {{hevel|futile}} and a grievous affliction.

A man may father a hundred children and live for many years; yet no matter how long he lives, if he is unsatisfied with his prosperity and does not even receive a proper burial, I say that a stillborn child is better off than he.
For a stillborn child enters in {{hevel|futility}} and departs in darkness, and his name is shrouded in obscurity.
The child, though neither seeing the sun nor knowing anything, has more rest than that man,
even if he lives a thousand years twice over but fails to enjoy his prosperity. Do not all go to the same place?

units.label: The appetite and the future we cannot secure

verses.text: All a man’s {{amal|labor}} is for his mouth, yet his appetite is never satisfied.
What {{yitron|advantage}}, then, has the wise man over the fool? What gain comes to the poor man who knows how to conduct himself before others?
Better what the eye can see than the wandering of desire. This too is {{hevel|futile}} and a pursuit of the wind.
Whatever exists was named long ago, and it is known what man is; but he cannot contend with one stronger than he.
For the more words, the more {{hevel|futility}}, and how does that profit anyone?
For who knows what is good for a man during the few days in which he passes through his {{hevel|fleeting}} life like a shadow? Who can tell a man what will come after him under the sun?

hevel.gloss: Vapor, breath, mist, the drumbeat word of Ecclesiastes, translated 'futile,' 'futility,' or 'fleeting.' It names things as transient and impossible to grasp. A full barn with an empty heart is hevel: real, yet slipping away like breath, unable to be held or truly enjoyed.

amal.gloss: Toil, labor, the wearying effort of making a living. Here a man's whole amal goes 'for his mouth,' feeding an appetite that is never finally full, a picture of endless work chasing a satisfaction it cannot reach.

yitron.gloss: Gain, profit, advantage, the surplus left when the accounts are closed. The Teacher asks what real advantage the wise man has over the fool if both die and neither can secure the future; the honest answer under the sun is troublingly little.

basic.context: This chapter is the dark mirror of the joy the Teacher just commended. He describes a man who has it all: riches, wealth, honor, everything his heart could want. And then he names the tragedy: God has not granted him the power to enjoy any of it, and a stranger ends up enjoying it instead. This, he says, is a sickness that weighs on the whole human race. To make the point unforgettable, he says that a stillborn child, who never saw the sun, is actually better off than a man who lives twice a thousand years but cannot enjoy his prosperity. The chapter ends on our smallness: our appetite is never full, and we cannot see or control what comes next.
basic.hermeneutics: This is deliberately uncomfortable wisdom, and we should not soften it. The point is not that wealth is evil but that prosperity without the capacity to enjoy it is a living death. The capacity to enjoy is itself a gift only God can give, 5:19, and where it is absent, having everything counts for nothing. The chapter drives us to a humbling place: we cannot manufacture our own satisfaction, and we cannot contend with the God who is stronger than we are.
basic.originalLanguages: The word hevel, breath or vapor, ties the chapter together: full hands and an empty heart are 'futile,' the stillborn 'enters in futility,' many words breed more 'futility,' and life itself is a 'fleeting' shadow. A man's amal, his toil, is all 'for his mouth,' yet the appetite is never filled. And the Teacher's blunt question about yitron, advantage, exposes how little the wise man finally gains over the fool if neither can secure tomorrow.
basic.typology: The unsatisfied rich man whose goods pass to a stranger foreshadows Jesus' rich fool, whose barns and years profit him nothing at death. The never satisfied appetite points ahead to the deeper hunger only Christ can fill, the bread and water of life after which we never hunger or thirst again.
basic.christ: The tragedy of prosperity you cannot enjoy exposes a hunger no possession can fill, and Jesus answers it directly: whoever drinks the water He gives will never thirst again. He is the bread of life; the one who comes to Him will never be hungry. Where the Teacher shows that man 'cannot contend with one stronger than he,' the gospel shows the stronger One coming not to crush but to save, giving the satisfied heart that riches never could.
basic.god: God is sovereign over enjoyment itself. He gives riches to one man and withholds from him the power to enjoy them; He is the 'one stronger' with whom no one can contend. This is sobering, but it is also the ground of hope: if satisfaction is God's to give, then it can be sought from Him, and the Giver is better than every gift.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 12 tells of the rich fool whose goods went to others. Psalm 39 calls each person 'a mere breath.' John 6 and John 4 present Christ as the bread and water that finally satisfy. Job 1 echoes 'naked I came, naked I return.'
basic.oneStory: Prosperity without enjoyment is the sharpest proof that our hunger is too deep for this world to fill. The Teacher lets the ache stand unrelieved, and the whole Bible carries that ache toward Christ, the bread and water of life, the only one who can grant both the gift and the God given power to enjoy it, now and forever.

context.literary: Chapter 6 is the hinge and low point of the book's middle, deliberately answering the enjoyment passage of 5:18 to 20 with its opposite. It opens with a diagnosis, 6:1 to 2, sharpens into the extreme comparison with a stillborn child, 6:3 to 6, and closes with a cluster of sayings on the unfilled appetite and the unknowable future, 6:7 to 12, which forms a bridge into the proverbs of chapter 7.
context.historical: In the ancient world, many children, long life, and a proper burial were the marks of a blessed man, and losing one's estate to a 'stranger' rather than an heir was a bitter dishonor. The Teacher takes every visible sign of blessing and shows that without the inner gift of enjoyment they add up to nothing.
context.canonical: The chapter deepens Scripture's realism about wealth and mortality. Its 'mere breath' view of life echoes the Psalms, and its portrait of endless craving sets up the New Testament's answer in Christ, who alone satisfies the hunger that possessions expose but cannot meet.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that prosperity is worthless without the God given power to enjoy it, to press this truth to its most shocking form so the reader feels it, and to humble us before our own limits, the appetite we cannot fill and the future we cannot see or secure.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter is almost entirely diagnosis, not command. It describes a real and common misery and refuses to explain it away. The implied counsel is indirect: since enjoyment is God's gift and the future is beyond us, we are driven to seek satisfaction from God rather than from accumulation.

debates.question: Is 6:1-6 an outburst of despair, or a clear eyed diagnosis of prosperity without the God given power to enjoy?

views.label: It is despair, the book's bleakest note
views.case: Saying a stillborn child is better off than a man with a hundred children and long life sounds like raw pessimism, a cry that existence itself may not be worth it.

views.label: It is a diagnosis meant to redirect us
views.case: The comparison is a wisdom device, an extreme case that makes one point unmistakable: without the capacity to enjoy, given only by God, 5:19, even total prosperity is empty. It exposes the disease so we will seek the cure in God.

debates.bearing: Read as diagnosis, the passage is not the last word but a setup: it makes us feel the futility of accumulation so that the enjoyment God gives, and ultimately the satisfaction found in Christ, lands with full force.

debates.question: What does hevel mean when applied to a life like this?

views.label: Meaningless, life stripped of value
views.case: Translated 'vanity' or 'meaningless,' hevel can read as a verdict that such a life, and perhaps life in general, has no worth.

views.label: Fleeting and ungraspable, breath and shadow
views.case: Hevel means vapor or breath; 6:12 spells it out, life 'passes like a shadow.' The word names transience and elusiveness, not sheer worthlessness. The man's misery is that his fleeting days yield nothing he can hold or enjoy.

debates.bearing: Taking hevel as 'fleeting' keeps the Teacher coherent: life is brief and slippery, so the failure to enjoy God's gifts within it is a genuine tragedy, and the call is to receive satisfaction from God while the breath lasts.

study.originalLanguages: Hevel, breath or vapor, threads the whole chapter: possessions unenjoyed are 'futile,' the stillborn 'enters in futility,' more words breed more 'futility,' and life is a 'fleeting' shadow, 6:12. A man's amal, his toil, is all 'for his mouth,' feeding an appetite that is never filled. And the Teacher's cutting question about yitron, advantage, 6:8, exposes that neither wisdom nor wealth secures any surplus a person can carry past death, since 'he cannot contend with one stronger than he,' 6:10.

typology.type: The unsatisfied rich man whose goods pass to a stranger
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' rich fool, whose stored goods went to others the night he died, a warning to be rich toward God, Luke 12:16-21.

typology.type: The appetite that is never satisfied, 6:7
typology.fulfillment: The deep human hunger Christ alone fills as the bread of life and the giver of living water, John 6:35, John 4:14.
The New Testament call to fix our brief lives on the eternal, and the resurrection hope that outlasts the vapor, 2 Corinthians 4:17-18.

study.christ: The horror of this chapter is prosperity that cannot satisfy, a full life with an empty center. It uncovers a hunger no amount of possession can fill, and Jesus speaks directly into that emptiness: 'I am the bread of life; whoever comes to Me will never go hungry,' and 'whoever drinks the water I give will never thirst.' The Teacher's grim truth that a man 'cannot contend with one stronger than he' becomes gospel when the stronger One, the Lord Himself, comes not to overpower but to give life. Where accumulation leaves the heart starving and the future dark, Christ gives both the gift and the God granted power to enjoy it, and a future secured beyond the grave.
study.god: God is shown as sovereign even over the capacity for joy: He gives one man riches and withholds from him the power to enjoy them. He is 'one stronger' than any of us, with whom we cannot contend. This is a hard truth, yet it is not despairing, because it means satisfaction is God's to grant. The same God who withholds also gives, 5:19, and the chapter, by exposing how empty life is without His gift, quietly points us to seek it from His hand rather than from our own accumulating.

crossReferences.note: The rich fool who laid up goods for himself but was not rich toward God, and whose wealth passed to others at death.
Each person is 'a mere breath'; we heap up wealth not knowing who will gather it, the same hevel outlook.
Christ the bread of life, in whom the never satisfied appetite of 6:7 is finally met.
The water Christ gives becomes a spring welling up to eternal life, so that one never thirsts again.

study.oneStory: A man with everything and no power to enjoy it is Scripture's sharpest proof that our hunger runs deeper than this world can reach. The Teacher refuses to relieve the ache, and the Bible carries it forward until Christ answers it: the bread of life and the living water, the stronger One who comes to satisfy rather than to crush, giving both the gift and the joy of it, now and beyond the fleeting shadow of our days.

quiz.q: What is the 'evil' or affliction the Teacher names in 6:1-2?

options.text: A man has riches, wealth, and honor but is not granted the power to enjoy them, and a stranger enjoys them instead.
options.note: The tragedy is prosperity without the God given ability to enjoy it.

options.text: A wealthy man loses everything and dies in poverty.
options.note: Loss appears elsewhere, but here the man keeps his wealth and still cannot enjoy it.

options.text: A poor man is oppressed by officials.
options.note: That was chapter 5; here the subject is an unenjoyed abundance.

options.text: A man refuses to work and so has nothing.
options.note: The man lacks nothing his heart desires; the problem is the missing gift of enjoyment.

quiz.q: Why does the Teacher say a stillborn child is better off than the unsatisfied rich man?

options.text: Because the child has rest, while the man, for all his years and children, never enjoys his prosperity.
options.note: The extreme comparison drives home that a life unenjoyed is worse than one never lived.

options.text: Because both finally go to the same place.
options.note: Verse 6 says this, but the sharper point is the man's failure to enjoy what he has.

options.text: Because the child avoids the oppression of officials.
options.note: That theme belongs to chapter 5, not this comparison.

options.text: Because children are always better off than adults.
options.note: The comparison is specific and shocking, not a general claim about children.

quiz.q: What does 6:7-12 say about human limits?

options.text: Our appetite is never satisfied, and we cannot see or secure what comes after us under the sun.
options.note: The closing verses stress the unfilled hunger and the unknowable future.

options.text: The wise man clearly outgains the fool in every way.
options.note: The text actually questions how much advantage the wise man really has, 6:8.

options.text: Hard work always leads to lasting satisfaction.
options.note: Verse 7 says the opposite: labor feeds an appetite never filled.

options.text: We can fully control our own future if we plan well.
options.note: Verse 12 says no one can tell what will come after him under the sun.

quiz.q: Is 6:1-6 best read as pure despair or as a diagnosis?

options.text: As a diagnosis: an extreme case exposing that prosperity is empty without the God given power to enjoy it.
options.note: The shocking comparison is a wisdom device to make us feel the disease and seek the cure in God.

options.text: As the book's bleakest and most pessimistic note.
options.note: It is genuinely dark, but read as diagnosis it sets up the gift of enjoyment and the satisfaction found in God.

options.text: As advice never to have children or wealth.
options.note: The passage does not counsel against children or wealth; it targets the inability to enjoy them.

options.text: As proof that life has no meaning whatsoever.
options.note: The Teacher elsewhere commends real joy; this passage exposes a specific tragedy, not universal meaninglessness.

quiz.q: How does this chapter point us toward Christ?

options.text: It uncovers a hunger no wealth can fill, which Christ alone satisfies as the bread of life and living water.
options.note: The never satisfied appetite finds its answer in Jesus, John 6:35 and John 4:14.

options.text: It reminds us that life is short, as the gospel also teaches.
options.note: True, but it underplays the direct line to Christ who satisfies the deepest hunger.

options.text: It promises that the faithful will always be wealthy.
options.note: The chapter runs the other way, exposing the emptiness of unenjoyed wealth.

options.text: It has nothing to do with Christ.
options.note: Its theme of unsatisfied hunger runs straight to Jesus, the bread and water of life.

questions: The Teacher pictures a man with everything who cannot enjoy any of it. Have you ever reached a goal and felt strangely empty when you got there? What did that emptiness reveal?
Enjoyment, the passage insists, is a gift only God gives. Where do you need to ask God not just for more, but for the ability to delight in what you already have?
The man's appetite was 'never satisfied.' What hunger in you keeps outrunning every attempt to feed it, and what might it be pointing you toward?
Verse 12 admits we cannot see what comes after us. How does it steady you, rather than frighten you, to leave the unknown future in the hands of the God who is stronger than you?
Jesus offers bread and water that finally satisfy. What would it look like this week to bring your deepest hunger to Him instead of to your possessions or achievements?

translationNotes.note: 'Futile' renders hevel, breath or vapor; the misery is not that the wealth is worthless but that it slips through the man's grasp unenjoyed, like mist. 'A stranger will enjoy them' means an outsider rather than an heir, a bitter dishonor in that culture.
The BSB reads 'the more futility, and how does that profit anyone,' where the source used a dash; here it is set off with a comma. The line mocks the idea that piling up words secures anything.
'Fleeting life like a shadow' again renders hevel and its imagery. 'Under the sun' is the Teacher's phrase for life viewed within this world alone, apart from what God reveals of eternity.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Ecclesiastes 6:1-12. Verse content segments concatenated in order; poetic lines rendered as flowing prose within each verse. Em dash in 6:11 replaced with a comma. No em or en dashes used anywhere.
